quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK MISCELLANEOUS APHORISMS; THE SOUL OF MAN ***
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: MISCELLANEOUS APHORISMS; THE SOUL OF MAN
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: London Arthur L. Humphreys 1911
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: (Miscellaneous aphorisms, followed by The Soul of Man.)
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: The mystery of love is greater than the mystery of death.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Women are made to be loved, not to be understood.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: It is absurd to have a hard and fast rule about what one should read and what one shouldn't. Moren than half of modern culture depends on what one shouldn't read.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Women, as someone says, love with their ears, just as men love with their eyes, if they ever love at all.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: It is better to be beautiful than to be good, but it is better to be good than to be ugly.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Nothing looks so like innocence as an indiscretion.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Misfortunes one can endure, they come from outside, they are accidents. But to suffer for one's faults
author: -ah! there is the sting of life.

quote: Beauty is the only thing that time cannot harm. Philosophies fall away like sand, creeds follow one another, but what is beautiful is a joy for all seasons, a possession for all eternity.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Questions are never indiscreet; answers sometimes are.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Twenty years of romance make a woman look like a ruin; but twenty years of marriage make her something like a public building.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: The only thing that one really knows about human nature is that it changes.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Anyone can sympathise with the sufferings of a friend, but it requires a very fine nature to sympathise with a friend's success.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Selfishness is not living as one wishes to live, it is asking others to live as one wishes to live: and unselfishness is letting other people's lives alone, not interfering with them.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: A man who does not think for himself does not think at all.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Nowadays people seem to look on life as a speculation. It is not a speculation. It is a sacrament. Its ideal is love. Its purification is sacrifice.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: All sympathy is fine, but sympathy with suffering is the least fine mode.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: If you pretend to be good the world takes you very seriously. If you pretend to be bad it doesn't. Such is the astounding stupidity of optimism.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: It is most dangerous nowadays for a husband to pay any attention to his wife in public. It always makes people think that he beats her when they're alone. The world has grown so suspicious of anything that looks like a happy married life.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Actors are so fortunate. They can choose whether they will appear in tragedy or in comedy, whether they will suffer or make merry, laugh or shed tears. But in real life it is different. Most men and women are forced to perform parts for which they have no qualifications. The world is a stage, but the play is badly cast.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Men know life too early; women know life too late
author: that is the difference between men and women.

quote: He who stands most remote from his age is he who mirrors it best.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: There is only one thing in the world worse than being talked about, and that is not being talked about.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Life is not governed by will or intention. Life is a question of nerves and fibres and slowly built
author: up cells, in which thought hides itself and passion has its dreams.

quote: Man is a being with myriad lives and myriad sensations, a complex, multiform creature that bears within itself strange legacies of thought and passion, and whose very flesh is tainted with the monstrous maladies of the dead.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: As long as a woman can look ten years younger than her own daughter she is perfectly satisfied.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: There is always something infinitely mean about other people's tragedies.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Public and private life are different things. They have different laws and move on different lines.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: When one is placed in the position of guardian one has to adopt a very high moral tone on all subjects. It's one's duty to do so.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: I have always been of opinion that a man who desires to get married should know either everything or nothing.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: An engagement should come on a young girl as a surprise, pleasant or unpleasant, as the case may be. It is hardly a matter that she could be allowed to arrange for herself.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: If the lower classes don't set us a good example what on earth is the use of them? They seem, as a class, to have absolutely no sense of moral responsibility.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: If a woman cannot make her mistakes charming she is only a female.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: The world was made for men and not for women.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: It is always with the best intentions that the worst work is done.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: If you wish to understand others you must intensify your own individualism.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Why do you talk so trivially about life? Because I think that life is far too important a thing ever to talk seriously about it.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: What a pity that in life we only get our lessons when they are of no use to us.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: It is better to have a permanent income than to be fascinating.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Relations are simply a tedious pack of people who haven't got the remotest knowledge of how to live nor the smallest instinct about when to die.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Charity creates a multitude of sins.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: My experience is that as soon as people are old enough to know better they don't know anything at all.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Truth is a very complex thing and politics is a very complex business. There are wheels within wheels. One may be under certain obligations to people that one must pay. Sooner or later in political life one has to compromise. Everyone does.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: Men can love what is beneath them
author: -things unworthy, stained, dishonoured. We women worship when we love; and when we lose our worship we lose everything.

quote: The proper basis for marriage is a mutual misunderstanding.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: The one advantage of playing with fire is that one never gets even singed. It is the people who don't know how to play with it who get burned up.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: There are moments when one has to choose between living one's own life fully, entirely, completely, or dragging out some false, shallow, degrading existence that the world in its hypocrisy demands.
author: Wilde, Oscar

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK MAXIMS FOR REVOLUTIONISTS ***
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Maxims for Revolutionists
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Do not do unto others as you would that they should do unto you. Their tastes may not be the same.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Never resist temptation: prove all things: hold fast that which is good.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Do not love your neighbor as yourself. If you are on good terms with yourself it is an impertinence: if on bad, an injury.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The golden rule is that there are no golden rules.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The art of government is the organization of idolatry.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The bureaucracy consists of functionaries; the aristocracy, of idols; the democracy, of idolaters.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The populace cannot understand the bureaucracy: it can only worship the national idols.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The savage bows down to idols of wood and stone: the civilized man to idols of flesh and blood.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: A limited monarchy is a device for combining the inertia of a wooden idol with the credibility of a flesh and blood one.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: When the wooden idol does not answer the peasant's prayer, he beats it: when the flesh and blood idol does not satisfy the civilized man, he cuts its head off.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: He who slays a king and he who dies for him are alike idolaters.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Kings are not born: they are made by artificial hallucination. When the process is interrupted by adversity at a critical age, as in the case of Charles II, the subject becomes sane and never completely recovers his kingliness.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The Court is the servant's hall of the sovereign.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Vulgarity in a king flatters the majority of the nation.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The flunkeyism propagated by the throne is the price we pay for its political convenience.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: If the lesser mind could measure the greater as a foot
author: rule can measure a pyramid, there would be finality in universal suffrage. As it is, the political problem remains unsolved.

quote: Democracy substitutes election by the incompetent many for appointment by the corrupt few.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Democratic republics can no more dispense with national idols than monarchies with public functionaries.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Government presents only one problem: the discovery of a trustworthy anthropometric method.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Excess of insularity makes a Briton an Imperialist.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Excess of local self
author: assertion makes a colonist an Imperialist.

quote: A colonial Imperialist is one who raises colonial troops, equips a colonial squadron, claims a Federal Parliament sending its measures to the Throne instead of to the Colonial Office, and, being finally brought by this means into insoluble conflict with the insular British Imperialist, "cuts the painter" and breaks up the Empire.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: He who confuses political liberty with freedom and political equality with similarity has never thought for five minutes about either.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Nothing can be unconditional: consequently nothing can be free.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Liberty means responsibility. That is why most men dread it.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The duke inquires contemptuously whether his gamekeeper is the equal of the Astronomer Royal; but he insists that they shall both be hanged equally if they murder him.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The notion that the colonel need be a better man than the private is as confused as the notion that the keystone need be stronger than the coping stone.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Where equality is undisputed, so also is subordination.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Equality is fundamental in every department of social organization.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The relation of superior to inferior excludes good manners.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: When a man teaches something he does not know to somebody else who has no aptitude for it, and gives him a certificate of proficiency, the latter has completed the education of a gentleman.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: A fool's brain digests philosophy into folly, science into superstition, and art into pedantry. Hence University education.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The vilest abortionist is he who attempts to mould a child's character.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: At the University every great treatise is postponed until its author attains impartial judgment and perfect knowledge. If a horse could wait as long for its shoes and would pay for them in advance, our blacksmiths would all be college dons.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: He who can, does. He who cannot, teaches.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: A learned man is an idler who kills time with study. Beware of his false knowledge: it is more dangerous than ignorance.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Activity is the only road to knowledge.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Every fool believes what his teachers tell him, and calls his credulity science or morality as confidently as his father called it divine revelation.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: No man fully capable of his own language ever masters another.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: No man can be a pure specialist without being in the strict sense an idiot.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Do not give your children moral and religious instruction unless you are quite sure they will not take it too seriously. Better be the mother of Henri Quatre and Nell Gwynne than of Robespierre and Queen Mary Tudor.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Marriage is popular because it combines the maximum of temptation with the maximum of opportunity.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Marriage is the only legal contract which abrogates as between the parties all the laws that safeguard the particular relation to which it refers.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The essential function of marriage is the continuance of the race, as stated in the Book of Common Prayer.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The accidental function of marriage is the gratification of the amoristic sentiment of mankind.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The artificial sterilization of marriage makes it possible for marriage to fulfill its accidental function whilst neglecting its essential one.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: The most revolutionary invention of the XIX century was the artificial sterilization of marriage.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: Any marriage system which condemns a majority of the population to celibacy will be violently wrecked on the pretext that it outrages morality.
author: Shaw, Bernard

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK ARABIAN WISDOM: SELECTIONS AND TRANSLATIONS FROM THE ARABIC ***
author: Wortabet, John

quote: The Wisdom of the East Series
author: Wortabet, John

quote: SELECTIONS AND TRANSLATIONS FROM THE ARABIC
author: Wortabet, John

quote: JOHN MURRAY, ALBEMARLE STREET
author: Wortabet, John

quote: What doth the Lord require of thee, but to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God!
author: -MICAH vi. 8.

quote: NORTHBROOK SOCIETY, 21, CROMWELL ROAD, KENSINGTON, S.W.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: [1] "A fool throws a stone into a well, and a thousand wise men cannot get it out."
author: Wortabet, John

quote: [2] "A man is safe when alone." "Paradise without human companions is not worth living in."
author: Wortabet, John

quote: [3] "Do no good, and you will meet with no evil."
author: Wortabet, John

quote: THE FIRST CHAPTER OF THE KORAN
author: Wortabet, John

quote: REPENTANCE, AND GOD'S FORGIVING MERCY
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Koran_. O ye who believe, repent unto God, for He loveth them who are penitent. O ye who believe in me, who by much sin have done a great wrong to themselves, despair not of the mercy of God, for He forgiveth all sins. Verily He forgiveth and is merciful.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Traditions_. Sorrow for sin is repentance. He who repents is like him who has not sinned.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Wise Sayings and Proverbs_. No intercession succeeds so effectually as repentance.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: The most truthful man is he who is true to his repentance.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Two sins only God does not forgive
author: -worship of false gods and injury to men.

quote: A SINNER'S CRY UNTO GOD[2]
author: Wortabet, John

quote: [2] The original Arabic is in verse.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: O Thou who knowest every thought, and hearest every cry, Who art the source of all that is, or ever shall be, Who art the only hope in every trouble, The only help in every plaint and every woe, Whose treasures of bounty and word creative are one, God of all good, hear my prayer!
author: Wortabet, John

quote: One sole plea I have
author: -my need of Thee; But needing Thee my need is filled. One only resource I have--to stand and knock; And if unheard at Thy mercy-gate, to whom shall I go?

quote: Whom shall I call, what Name shall I invoke, If Thy needy servant shall in vain Thy bounty seek? But far be it from Thee, God of grace, to refuse a sinner's cry. Too good and gracious art Thou to send me thus away.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Contrite, I stand at Thy door, Believing that contrite prayer availeth much with Thee. Suppliant, I stretch forth my hands, And with all my soul look up to Thee. Save me, God, from every ill, and be Thy favour ever mine!
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Koran_. God forgiveth past sins; let men forgive and pardon. Forgive freely. Forgiving others is the nearest thing to piety.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Traditions_. He who forgiveth others, God forgiveth him.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Be merciful, and you will have mercy; forgive and you will be forgiven.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Sayings and Proverbs_. Of all things God loveth best forgiveness when one is able to inflict harm, and forbearance when one is angry.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: The pleasure of forgiving is sweeter than the pleasure of revenge.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Forgiveness is perfect when the sin is not remembered.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: The most wicked of men is he who accepts no apology, covers no sin, and forgives no fault.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Small men transgress, great men forgive.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: A noble man condones and pardons, and when by chance he finds out a sin, he conceals it.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: A man said to another who had spoken evil of him: "If what you have said be true, may God forgive me; and if false, may He forgive you."
author: Wortabet, John

quote: CLEMENCY, FORBEARANCE, AND GENTLENESS
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Koran_. Those who worship the Merciful One are they who walk on the earth gently, and who, when fools speak to them, say "Peace." (25, 64.)
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Traditions_. Be friendly to him who would be unfriendly to you, give him who will not give you, and forbear with him who would do you harm.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Next to faith in God, the chief duty of man is to treat his fellow men with gentleness and courtesy.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: _Sayings and Proverbs_. Gentleness is one of the noblest traits in a man's character.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: A gentle man is a man of great beauty.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: One of the surest evidences of gentleness is tenderness to fools.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: The fierce anger of a foolish man is checked by gentleness as a fierce fire is extinguished by water.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Gentleness is sometimes an humiliation, and he who is always forbearing and patient may be trodden down by fools.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: If you honour a vile man, you disgrace the code of honour.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Humility is that line of conduct which is a mean between overbearing pride on the one hand and abject servility on the other, as economy is the middle term between extravagance and avarice.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: Humility is the crown of nobility, a ladder to honour, and a means of procuring love and esteem.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: He who humbleth himself, God lifteth him up.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: A wise man was once asked whether he knew of any good which is not coveted, or any evil which deserves no mercy, and he said: "Yes, they are humility and pride."
author: Wortabet, John

quote: To despise a proud man is true humility.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: True nobility lies in high character and refined manners, not in noble birth or ancient pedigree.
author: Wortabet, John

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK POPHILO: POPULAR PHILOSOPHY ***
author: Dom

quote: Copyright (C) 2002 by L.M. Wong Dommy dominaeprimus@yahoo.com
author: Dom

quote: THINKERS IN THEIR OWN WORDS DISPLAY AND CONVEY THE DEPTH OR SHALLOWNESS OF THEIR OWN AND SO SHALL BE PILLORIED OR ACCLAIMED BY THEIR OWN THOUGHTS.
author: Dom

quote: THERE IS IMMENSITY IN SPARSENESS AND SPARSENESS IN IMMENSITY.
author: Dom

quote: PoPHILO POPULAR PHILOSOPHY
author: Dom

quote: 1.Better to be a willing servant to our mind than an unwilling slave to a tyrant?s will.
author: Dom

quote: 2.The physical self and mind as willing servants to a worthy cause is a form of devotion. The physical self and mind as unwilling slaves to a despised cause is physical and spiritual violation.
author: Dom

quote: 3.Controlled freedom is externally induced refrain, discretion and responsibility .It is only when we are given the sovereignty of freewill do we realise our susceptibility and vulnerability.
author: Dom

quote: 4.If a soothsayer predicts negative events in our lives, use freewill to avoid them. If a soothsayer predicts positive events in our lives ,use freewill to fulfil them.
author: Dom

quote: 5.We should all generate our own knowledge not just absorb all that we have learned. In order to attain our full intellectual potential ,we need both the scholar and innovator in us.
author: Dom

quote: 6.When at last we realise how much we?ve lost when things have passed, we have grasped both wisdom and folly.
author: Dom

quote: 7.A pessimist is plagued by dark vision. An optimist is embraced by clear hope .A practical realist has both dark vision and clear hope .
author: Dom

quote: 8.Sweeping statements are uninsured ventures offering high risks of swallowing our words and pride.
author: Dom

quote: 9.The course of one?s life is like a quest for a masterpiece. It hinges on just the right intensity and apt strokes.
author: Dom

quote: 10.The best we thought we were at earlier times are sometimes not as good as the best stage we are at now. Being at one?s best depends on the time scale it is judged on. We peak differently at different times. All our peak points are the summits of their own time.
author: Dom

quote: 11.A mortal fact here : Those who live within the century they were born in and never get to see the next or those fortunate enough to see the next.
author: Dom

quote: 12.Clichs that we cannot do without are not clichs but language essentials.
author: Dom

quote: 13.Anxiety to the mind is akin to a fractured leg to the body.
author: Dom

quote: 14.To maintain constant levels of alertness, for the prolific mind ; repose while for the sedentary mind ; stimulation.
author: Dom

quote: 15.Moderate amounts of guidelines in convention and etiquette ensures that civilization behaves in civil fashion. Excessive amounts of convention and etiquette converts life into ritualistic enslavement of thought and action.
author: Dom

quote: 16.If you don?t know nothing you know something.
author: Dom

quote: 17.One insists because of truth. One insists because of prejudice.
author: Dom

quote: 18.Experience is when something becomes easier, more predictable with less surprises with our ability to discern those worth cherishing and those only worth during their fleeting hour.
author: Dom

quote: 19.Innovation may spell the demise of erstwhile techniques which were innovations of yesteryears or just muffle them. The possibility of revamps, revivals cannot be overruled entirely be they in similar, related or different fields of interest. Nostalgia has its charms.
author: Dom

quote: 20.In order to be profound in thoughts the mind must be liberated from the immediate reality of trifles.
author: Dom

quote: 21.The inexperienced and strong pour all their power to complete a task. The experienced and not as strong place their power where it?s needed.
author: Dom

quote: 22.There are those who are weak with strong will and those strong with weak will.
author: Dom

quote: 23.Sharing is to be aware that we are not the absolute owner and to attune ourselves to the pendulum cycle of holding and releasing.
author: Dom

quote: 24.Communication isn?t confined to mere expression. Communication involves discretion and sensitivity as well to connotations and subtleties.
author: Dom

quote: 25.A wizened head is not necessarily a wise one, vice
author: versa.

quote: 26.To be educated with what we know and to expect them in a particular form only is to be unprepared for exceptions.
author: Dom

quote: 27.Innovation brings changes to lifestyle , technology and attitude too.
author: Dom

quote: 28.To be comfortable with Falsehood is to be uneasy with Truth.
author: Dom

quote: 29.If conventional methods bind us in a knot , let innovation undo it.
author: Dom

quote: 30.If conformity fills the world, inventiveness shapes it.
author: Dom

quote: 31.If conformity follows the charted course, innovation plots it.
author: Dom

quote: 32.Tradition is a method to get fledglings and elders involved. For a while, regardless of seniority ,they are united by a common cause.
author: Dom

quote: 33.Daring, recklessness and folly are discerned by the odds, rational thought and degree of desperation.
author: Dom

quote: 34.Observe interests before resolving the conflict.
author: Dom

quote: 35.Meet your Maker with readiness and anticipation , honour and humility , knowledge and receptiveness ,forgiveness in soul , remorse for vices , joy for virtues and devoted willingness to be of service to the Omnipotent.
author: Dom

quote: 36.Some interpretations of morality and immorality change as humanity evolves while the absolutes remain.
author: Dom

quote: 37.We labour for ourselves and for others.
author: Dom

quote: 38.Trends are variants and possibilities. They dock at the harbours of fashion and drift away to the sea of pass. From obscurity sometimes they do return. Direction lies not there. Direction lies in rudiments upon which trends were moulded.
author: Dom

quote: 39.Unbridled discerning divides us.
author: Dom

quote: 40.To know much of everything is extraordinary. To know much of something or someone is a common norm.
author: Dom

quote: 41.Most of the time a
author: know-it-all is a know-less-than-all.

quote: 42.Having just adequate knowledge to exist is like being familiar with our own dwelling. But once outside ,we become ignorant strangers.
author: Dom

quote: 43.Let us live and understand not just mere pride from knowing about things.
author: Dom

quote: 44.Search within and discover that ...we know more than we think ...we know less than we think ...we are less than what we think we are ..we are greater than what we think we are
author: Dom

quote: 45.It is a terrible affliction to underestimate and overestimate ourselves frequently.
author: Dom

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK CRANKISMS ***
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Illustrations are explained at the end of the text.]
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: By Lisle de Vaux MATTHEWMAN
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Pictured By Clare Victor DWIGGINS
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: * MCMI * HENRY T. COATES & CO. PHILADELPHIA
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Copyright, 1901, by Henry T. Coates & Company. _All rights reserved._
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: If I may be permitted to offer a suggestion, the Crankisms should be read in the spirit in which sermons are listened to
author: -with the object of discovering whom they hit. This will furnish amusement, for what is more entertaining than trying the cap on others?

quote: The settings speak for themselves; but the author desires to express his indebtedness to the artist for having infused life into and lent grace to dead bones of words, and for having, in many cases, given to those words a deeper and more subtle meaning than they themselves could be made to express.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The kisses of an enemy are deceitful, but not as deceitful as the advice of the friend who is always counseling you for your own good.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The best and the worst in man respond only to woman's touch
author: -unfortunately for man.

quote: Men reason; women do not. Woman has no logic, and judging from the use it is to man, is better off without it.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The present arrangement of society refuses to many the means to live, while forbidding them the right to die when they wish.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Woman generally tries to attract a man's eye, and then blames him for being caught by prettiness and superficial charms. But she rarely tries to appeal to his better self.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The man who is pockmarked has most to say against freckles.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Charity covers a multitude of sins which are committed in her name.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Life is full of golden opportunities for doing what we do not want to do.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Never compliment a woman and you will earn her undying enmity. Respect is rarely appreciated by her; but compliments are always at a premium, even counterfeits being accepted as greedily as the real.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: When we grow old we walk unfeelingly over that which we, in our youth, madly chased.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The biggest fool is the one who thinks he can fool others with impunity without them knowing and resenting it.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: When we get what we want we are always disappointed to find that it is not what we wanted.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Like does not always worship like: Beauty often worships the Beast.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: We were all in the front row when modesty was served out
author: -at least we think so.

quote: Because some men are ruined by intemperance it does not follow that all should become abstainers, any more than because some men are ruined by marriage all men should remain single.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: What men see in women or women in men to admire is generally a puzzle to those who know the men and women in question intimately.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The only compliment which a woman really dislikes is that which is paid to another.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Things have changed since Shakespeare's time: men's evil deeds we write in sympathetic ink; their virtues on marble tombstones.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Our own weaknesses we regard as misfortunes from which we cannot escape; the weaknesses of others we consider crimes.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: No matter how well we do, we are sure to be anxious to impress upon others that what we have achieved is trifling
author: - compared with that of which we are capable.

quote: A woman is not a woman merely by reason of her sex, any more than an angel is of necessity an angel of light.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: We are quite able, while hating sin, to pity and be charitable to the sinner
author: -when we happen to be the sinner concerned.

quote: The commonly accepted idea that a woman of beauty is of necessity lacking in mental qualities, must have originated in the head of some woman who possessed neither.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The Devil is not as black as he is painted. In fact, he is more like us than we care to admit.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Faithful are the wounds of a friend; and as it is more blessed to give than to receive, we prefer to do the wounding.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The naked truth and a naked lie Are shocking alike to society.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: A man often envies another man his physical qualities
author: -rarely his mental. As we have no soul mirror we cannot see the reflection of our spiritual deformities.

quote: It is easy to have conscientious scruples when they are profitable.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The man who marries for money is a fool, but rarely as big a fool as he who marries for love.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: When you have done a man a favor do not insist too earnestly that it is a mere trifle, or he may take you at your word and not trouble to repay it; which would be very disappointing.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: The gentle art of making enemies is the one natural accomplishment which is common to all sorts and conditions of men
author: -and women.

quote: What we think of ourselves combined with what others think of us is a very fair estimate.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: If a girl cannot make up her mind between two men it is because she has no mind worth making up.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Besides, any man who will knowingly be one of two is not worth the trouble of thinking about.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: If we devoted as much attention to our own affairs as we freely give to those of others, we and others would be gainers.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Merit, like the show inside a circus, is of comparatively little use as a drawing card; it is the bluff and buncombe the banging drum and megaphone of the barker which is the successful magnet.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: We always know what we should do under certain circumstances, but unfortunately we never find circumstances arranged so as to suit what we do.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: An over sensitive conscience is simply the evidence of spiritual dyspepsia. The man who has it is no better than his fellows.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: Generosity, as commonly understood, consists in forcing upon others that for which one has no use.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: There is a greater difference between really thinking and only thinking that we think than most of us think.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

quote: We rashly demand that the devil shall have his due, forgetting that if that gentleman gets all that is coming to him it will go badly with some of us.
author: Matthewman, Lisle de Vaux

author: mot. _A word to the wise._

quote: A celui qui a son pâté au four on peut donner de son gâteau. _To one who has a pie in the oven you may give a bit of your cake._
author: Unknown

quote: A chacun son fardeau pèse. _Every one feels his own burden heavy._
author: Unknown

quote: A chair de loup sauce de chien. _For wolf’s flesh dog sauce._
author: Unknown

quote: A chaque fou plaît sa marotte. _Every fool likes his bauble._
author: Unknown

quote: A chaque jour suffit sa peine. _Sufficient for the day is the evil thereof._
author: Unknown

quote: A chaque saint son cierge. _To every saint his candle._
author: Unknown

quote: A chemin battu ne croît point d’herbe. _No grass grows on a beaten road._

quote: Acheter chat en poche. _To buy a cat in a poke._
author: Unknown

quote: A cheval donné, il ne faut point regarder à la bouche. _Look not a gift horse in the mouth._
author: Unknown

quote: A chose faite conseil pris. _When a thing is done advice comes too late._
author: Unknown

quote: A confesseurs, médecins, avocats, la vérité ne cèle de ton cas. _From confessors, doctors, and lawyers, do not conceal the truth of your case._
author: Unknown

quote: Adieu paniers, vendanges sont faites. _Farewell baskets, the vintage is ended._
author: Unknown

quote: A dur âne dur aiguillon. _For a stubborn ass a hard goad._
author: Unknown

quote: A femme avare galant escroc. _A covetous woman deserves a swindling gallant._
author: Unknown

quote: A force de mal aller tout ira bien. _By dint of going wrong all will come right._
author: Unknown

quote: A fripon fripon et demi. _To a rogue a rogue and a half._
author: Unknown

quote: A goupil endormi rien ne lui chet en gueule. _Nothing falls into the mouth of a sleeping fox._
author: Unknown

quote: A homme hardi fortune tend la main. _To a bold man fortune holds out her hand._
author: Unknown

quote: Aimer et savoir n’ont même manière. _To love and to be wise are two different things._
author: Unknown

quote: Ainsi dit le renard des mûres, quand il n’en peut avoir: elles ne sont point bonnes. _The fox says of the mulberries when he cannot get at them: they are not good at all._

quote: Aisé à dire est difficile à faire. _Easy to say is hard to do._
author: Unknown

quote: A la chandelle la chèvre semble demoiselle. _By candle
author: light a goat looks like a lady._

quote: A la guerre comme à la guerre. _At the wars as they do at the wars._
author: Unknown

quote: A l’amour et au feu on s’habitue. _One grows used to love and to fire._
author: Unknown

quote: A la presse vont les fous. _Fools go in throngs._
author: Unknown

quote: A la queue gît le venin. _In the tail lies the venom._
author: Unknown

author: Bogardus, C. A.

quote: In as much as the former editions of this book have been so well liked, excepting the type being so small, it has been mentioned by many that a correction in that particular would be well. I have revised and enlarged the book and it now appears from larger type.
author: Bogardus, C. A.

quote: Department number two treats on social questions that are now knocking at humanity's intellectual threshold for admission and solution.
author: Bogardus, C. A.

quote: From the time I was twelve years old I was considered a very fine shot with a rifle, although I did but very little shooting, and, in fact, did not know that I was any more than a common marksman; yet in any contests while a boy I always won.
author: Bogardus, C. A.

quote: I have thrown an object into the air and hit it 12 times before it struck the ground, I using an automatic shot gun.
author: Bogardus, C. A.

quote: C. A. BOGARDUS, _Champion Quick Shot of the World._
author: Bogardus, C. A.

quote: SPECIFIC INFLAMMATORY RHEUMATISM.
author: -One oz. Salt Petre, pulverized; one pint Sweet Oil. Bathe the parts affected three times a day with this mixture and a speedy cure will be the result.

quote: A SURE CURE FOR PILES.
author: -Confection of Senna, two ozs., Cream of Tartar one oz., Sulphur one oz., Syrup of Ginger, enough to make a stiff paste; mix. A piece as large as a nut is to be taken as often as necessary to keep the bowels open. One of the best remedies known.

quote: CURE FOR SORE THROAT IN ALL ITS DIFFERENT FORMS.
author: -Two ozs. Cayenne Pepper, one oz. common Salt, one-half pint of Vinegar. Warm over a slow fire and gargle the throat and mouth every hour. Garlic and Onion poultice applied to the outside. Castor Oil, one spoonful to keep the bowels open.

quote: CATARRH, POSITIVE CURE.
author: -Carbolic Acid, ten to twenty drops; Vaseline, one to two ozs. Mix and use with an atomizer 3 or 4 times per day.

quote: TO STRENGTHEN AND INVIGORATE THE SYSTEM.
author: -Two drms. Essential Salt of the Round Leaf Cornel, one scru. Extract Rhubarb, one scru. Ginger Powder. Make into pills, and take for a dose 2 or 3 twice a day.

quote: GREEN MOUNTAIN SALVE.
author: -Take one pound Beeswax, one pound of soft Butter, and one and one-half pounds soft Turpentine, twelve ounces Balsam Fir. Melt and strain. Use to heal fresh wounds, burns, scalds and all bad sores.

quote: A CURE FOR CANCER (AS USED BY A NEW YORK PHYSICIAN WITH GREAT SUCCESS).
author: -Take Red Oak Bark, and boil it to the thickness of molasses, then mix with sheep's tallow of equal proportion. Spread it on leaves of Linnwood green, and keep the plaster over the ulcer. Change once in eight hours.

quote: BOSCHEE'S GERMAN SYRUP.
author: -Wine of Tar two ozs., Fluid Extract Squills one oz., Tinct. Opium two drms., Fluid Extract Sanguinarie two drms., Syrup of Sugar eight ozs. Mix.

quote: HARTER'S IRON TONIC.
author: -Calisaya Bark two ozs., Citrate of Iron two ozs., Gentian two ozs., Cardamon Seed two ozs., Syrup two ozs., Alcohol two ozs., Water eight ozs. Mix.

quote: HALL'S BALSAM FOR THE LUNGS.
author: -Fluid Extract Ipecac one-half oz., Fluid Extract Squills one oz., Chloroform one-fourth oz., Wine of Tar one oz., Tinct. Opium, one-fifth oz., Fluid Extract of Mullen one oz., Syrup enough to make one pint.

quote: HALL'S HONEY OF HOARHOUND AND TAR.
author: -Wine of Tar one oz., Fluid Extract of Hoarhound one oz., Tinct. Opium one drm., Syrup Orange Peel one-half oz., Honey three ozs., Syrup enough to make one pint.

quote: HOOD'S SARSAPARILLA.
author: -Fluid Extract Sarsaparilla one oz., Fluid Extract Yellow Dock one oz., Fluid Extract Poke Root, one-half oz., Iodide of Potash one-half oz., Syrup Orange Peel one oz., Alcohol four ozs., Syrup enough to make one pint.

quote: HAMLIN'S WIZARD OIL.
author: -Oil Sassafras two ozs., Oil Cedar one oz., Gum Camphor one oz., Sulph. Ether two ozs., Chloroform two ozs., Tinct. Capsicum one oz., Aqua Ammonia two ozs., Oil Turpentine one oz., Tinct. Quassia three ozs., Alcohol half a gallon. Mix and you have a fine liniment.

quote: JAYNE'S EXPECTORANT.
author: -Syrup Squills two ozs., Tinct. Tolu one oz., Spirits Camphor one drm., Tinct. Digitalis one drm., Tinct. Lobelia one drm., Wine of Ipecac two drms., Tinc. Opium two drms., Antimonia two grains. Mix.

quote: JAYNE'S TONIC VERMIFUGE.
author: -L. santonnie twenty grs., Fluid Extract Pink Root three drms., Fluid Extract Senna two drms., Simple Elixir two ozs., Syrup two ozs. Mix. Take tablespoonful night and morning.

quote: AYER'S CHERRY PECTORAL.
author: -Take four grains of Acetate of Morphia, two fluid drachms of Tincture of Bloodroot, three fluid drachms each of Antimonial Wine and Wine of Ipecacuanha, and three fluid ounces Syrup of Wild Cherry. Mix.

quote: BROWN'S BRONCHIAL TROCHES.
author: -Take one pound pulverized Extract of Licorice, one and one-half pounds Pulverized Sugar, four ounces pulverized Cubebs, four ounces pulverized Gum Arabic, and one ounce of pulverized Extract of Conium. Mix.

quote: SUCCUS ALTERNS (McDADE'S).
author: -Fluid Extract Starlinga one oz., Fluid Extract Sarsaparilla one oz., Fluid Extract Phytolacca Decandra one-half oz., Fluid Extract Lappa Minor one oz., Fluid Extract Xanthoxylon one-half oz., Syrup fourteen ozs., Mix. Teaspoonful three times a day.

quote: SEVEN SEALS OF GOLDEN WONDER.
author: -Oil Cajeput two drms., Sassafras one-half oz., Oil Organum one drm., Oil Hemlock one drm., Oil Cedar one drm., Tincture Capsicum one-fourth oz., Alcohol enough to make one pint.

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK PROVERBS OF ALL NATIONS, COMPARED, EXPLAINED, AND ILLUSTRATED ***
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: A list of the changes made can be found at the end of the book. Formatting and special characters are indicated as follows:
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: EXPLAINED, AND ILLUSTRATED.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: Even the best proverb, though often the expression of the widest experience in the choicest language, can be thoroughly misapplied. It cannot embrace the whole of the subject, and apply in all cases like a mathematical formula. Its wisdom lies in the ear of the hearer.
author: -FRIENDS IN COUNCIL.

quote: LONDON: W. KENT & CO. (LATE D. BOGUE), 86, FLEET STREET, AND PATERNOSTER ROW. 1859.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: WINCHESTER: PRINTED BY HUGH BARCLAY, HIGH STREET.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: ENGLISH LITERATURE, in most departments the richest in Europe, is yet the only one in which there has hitherto existed no comprehensive collection of proverbs adapted to general use. To supply this deficiency is the object of the present attempt.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: My own experience in this department of literature is entirely in accordance with these views. I have, therefore, during the preparation of the following pages, kept constantly before my mind the Dean of Westminster's precise statement of things to be done, and things to be avoided.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: WOMEN, LOVE, MARRIAGE, ETC.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =What's sauce for the goose is sauce for the gander.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: This is an Englishwoman's proverb. The Italian sisterhood complain that "In men every mortal sin is venial; in women every venial sin is mortal."[1] These are almost the only proverbs relating to women in which justice is done to them, all the rest being manifestly the work of the unfair sex.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =If a woman were as little as she is good, A peascod would make her a gown and a hood.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: "The crab of the wood is sauce very good For the crab of the sea; But the wood of the crab is sauce for a drab, That will not her husband obey."
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =A spaniel, a woman, and a walnut tree,= =The more they're beaten the better they be.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: There is Latin authority for this barbarous distich.[7] The Italians say, "Women, asses, and nuts require rough hands."[8] Much wiser is the Scotch adage,
=Ye may ding the deil into a wife, but ye'll ne'er ding him out o' her.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =It's nae mair ferlie to see a woman greet than to see a guse gang barefit.=
author: -_Scotch._

quote: =A woman's mind and winter wind change oft.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: Women are variable as April weather" (German).[16] "Women, wind, and fortune soon change" (Spanish).[17] Francis I. of France wrote one day with a diamond on a window of the château of Chambord,
"Souvent femme varie: Bien fou qui s'y fie."
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: "A woman changes oft: Who trusts her is right soft."
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =A woman's tongue wags like a lamb's tail.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =A woman's strength is in her tongue.=
author: -_Welsh._

quote: =Arthur could not tame a woman's tongue.=
author: -_Welsh._

quote: "Three women and three geese make a market,"[18] according to the Italians. "Foxes are all tail, and women are all tongue;" at least, it is so in Auvergne.[19] "All women are good Lutherans," say the Danes; "they would rather preach than hear mass."[20] "A woman's tongue is her sword, and she does not let it rust," is a saying of the Chinese.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =Swine, women, and bees are not to be turned.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: ="Because" is a woman's answer.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: And not so unmeaning an answer as flippant critics imagine. It is an example of that much
author: admired figure of speech, aposiopesis, and means--because I will have it so. "What a woman wills, God wills" (French).[21] "Whatever a woman will she can" (Italian).[22]

quote: "The man's a fool who thinks by force or skill To stem the torrent of a woman's will; For if she will, she will, you may depend on't, And if she won't, she won't, and there's an end on't."
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =A woman conceals what she does not know.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =Women and bairns lein [conceal] what they kenna.=
author: -_Scotch._

quote: To a woman and a magpie tell what you would speak in the market
author: place" (Spanish).[25] Hotspur says to his wife,--

quote: "Constant you are, But yet a woman, and for secrecy No lady closer; for I well believe Thou wilt not utter what thou dost not know, And so far I will trust thee, gentle Kate."
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: But, if there is truth in proverbs, men have no right to reproach women for blabbing. A woman can at least keep her own secret. Try her on the subject of her age.
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =Beauty draws more than oxen.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: One hair of a woman draws more than a bell
author: rope" (German).[26]

quote: "And beauty draws us with a single hair."
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =Beauty is no inheritance.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: In spite of these curmudgeon maxims, let no fair maid despair whose face is her fortune, for "She that is born a beauty is born married" (Italian).[27]
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =Beauty is but skin deep.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =Fine feathers make fine fowls.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: Therefore, "If you want a wife choose her on Saturday, not on Sunday" (Spanish);[28] _i.e._, choose her in undress. "No woman is ugly when she is dressed" (Spanish);[29] at least, she is not so in her own opinion. "The swarthy dame, dressed fine, decries the fair one" (Spanish).[30]
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =The fairer the hostess the fouler the reckoning.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: A handsome landlady is bad for the purse" (French);[31] for this among other reasons
author: -that "If the landlady is fair, the wine too is fair" (German).[32]

quote: =A bonny bride is sune buskit.=
author: -_Scotch._

quote: =Joan is as good as my lady in the dark.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: =When candles are out all cats are grey.=
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: "Blemishes are unseen by night,"[33] says an ancient Latin proverb; and the Greeks held that "When the lamp is removed all women are alike."[34] Opinions may differ on that point, but all agree that
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: "The night Shows stars and women in a better light."
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: Hence the Italian warning to choose "Neither jewel, nor woman, nor linen by candlelight;"[35] and the French hyperbole, "By candlelight a goat looks a lady."[36]
author: Kelly, Walter K. (Walter Keating)

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK THE TRIADS OF IRELAND ***
author: Unknown

quote: [Transcriber's note: Linenotes and Footnotes moved as close as possible to their applicable entry to facilitate readability.]
author: Unknown

quote: DUBLIN HODGES, FIGGIS, & CO., LTD. LONDON: WILLIAMS & NORGATE 1906
author: Unknown

quote: _Printed by_ PONSONBY & GIBBS, _Dublin University Press_
author: Unknown

quote: TEXT AND TRANSLATION, 1
GLOSSES AND NOTES, 36
The collection of Irish Triads, which is here edited and translated for the first time, has come down to us in the following nine manuscripts, dating from the fourteenth to the nineteenth century:
=L=, _i.e._ the Yellow Book of Lecan, a vellum of the end of the fourteenth century, pp. 414_b_
author: -418_a_, a complete copy.

quote: =B=, _i.e._ the Book of Ballymote, a vellum of the end of the fourteenth century, pp. 65_b_
author: 66_b_ (ends imperfectly).

quote: [1] By an oversight I have referred to this MS. sometimes by Lec and sometimes by H. In some cases both Lec and H will be found quoted in the variants. The same MS. is always meant.
author: Unknown

quote: [2] As appears from the following colophon on p. 101: 'Oraoit uaim ar do lebor a hOedh in c_ed_luan iar n
author: aurtach Johannes. Baile Tibhaird ar bla maige mo mendad scribne hi farrad Se(a)ain hi Maoilconari. Mese (Dubthach) do scrib in ball soin da derpiris [et] rlae. Anno domini 1575. Guroiuh maith ag_a_t.

quote: Another copy, written in 1836 by Peter O'Longan, formerly in the possession of the Earls of Crawford, now belongs to the Rylands Library, Manchester, where it was found by Professor Strachan, who kindly copied a page or two for me. It is evidently a very corrupt copy which I have not thought worth the trouble of collating.
author: Unknown

quote: Lastly, there is in the Advocates' Library a copy in a vellum manuscript marked Kilbride III. It begins on fo. 9_b_^2 as follows:
author: -'Treching breath annso. Ceann Eirind Ardmacha.' I hope to collate it before long, and give some account of it in the next number of this series.

quote: Three kinds of martyrdom that are counted as a cross to man, _i.e._ white martyrdom, green martyrdom, and red martyrdom.
author: -The Cambray Homily (_Thesaurus Palaeohibernicus_, II., p. 246).

quote: Three enemies of the soul: the world, the devil, and an impious teacher.
author: -Colman maccu Beognae's Alphabet of Piety (_Zeitschrift fuer celtische Philologie_, III., p. 452).

quote: Three things whereby the devil shows himself in man: by his face, by his gait, by his speech.
author: -_Ib._, p. 453.

quote: Three profitable labours in the day: praying, working, reading.
author: -Regula Choluimb Cille (_Zeitschr._, III., p. 29).

quote: Three laymen of Ireland who became monks: Beccan son of Cula, Mochu son of Lonan, and Enda of Arann.
author: -Notes on the Felire of Oengus (Henry Bradshaw Society, vol. xxix., p. 112).

quote: Three chief artisans of Ireland: Tassach with Patrick, Conlaed with Brigit, and Daig with Ciaran.
author: -_Ib._, p. 186.

quote: Three poets of the world: Homer of the Greeks, Vergil of the Latins, Ruman of the Gaels.
author: -Book of Leinster, p. 354_b_.

quote: The three worst counsels that have been acted on in Ireland through the advice of saints: the cutting short of Ciaran's life, the banishment of Colum Cille, the expulsion of Mochuta from Rathen.
author: -Notes on the Felire of Oengus, p. 204, and Tripartite Life, p. 557.[3]

quote: [3] Where for 'wrong stories' read 'wrong counsels' (_sanasa saeba_).
author: Unknown

quote: This triad is thus versified in the Brussels MS. 5100:
Teora saoba sanasa Leithe Cuind roc[h]aras
author: [s]a: Mochuda cona clamhra[i]d d'ionnarba a Rathain roghlain, cur Coluim Cille tar sal, timdibhe saeghail Ciarain.

quote: Three things there are for which the Son of living God is not grateful: haughty piety, harsh reproof, reviling a person if it is not certain.[4]
author: Unknown

quote: [4] LB., p. 225 marg. inf., and Brussels MS. 5100, fo. 86_a_:
author: Unknown

quote: Fuil tri ni (a tri Br.) dona (danach Br.) buidech mac De bi: crabud uallach, coisced (coiccsed Br.) serb, ecnach duine mad inderb.
author: Unknown

quote: Three things there are for which the King of the sun is grateful: union of brethren, upright conversation, serving at the altar of God.[5]
author: Unknown

quote: [5] Edinburgh MS. xl, p. 28, and Brussels MS. 5100, fo. 86_a_:
author: Unknown

quote: Fuil treide dianab buidech ri greine: oenta brathar, comrad (fodail Ed.) cert, altoir De do thimthirecht.
author: Unknown

quote: Woe to the three folk in horrid hell of great blasts: folk who practise poetry, folk who violate their orders, mercenaries.[6]
author: Unknown

quote: [6] LB., p. 236, marg. inf.:
author: Unknown

quote: Mairg na tri lucht a n
author: iffirn uathmar anside: oes dogni dan, oes choilles grad, oes amsaine.

quote: Three things there are which do not behove the poor of living God: ingratitude for his life whatever it be, grumbling, and flattery.[7]
author: Unknown

quote: [7] LB., p. 238, marg. inf.:
author: Unknown

quote: Fuil tri ni na dlegair do bocht De bi: dimmda da bethaid cipe, cesacht ocus aibele.
author: Unknown

quote: The following modern triads I owe to a communication from Dr. P.W. Joyce, who heard them in his youth among the people of Limerick:
Three things to be distrusted: a cow's horn, a dog's tooth, and a horse's hoof.
author: Unknown

quote: Three disagreeable things at home: a scolding wife, a squalling child, and a smoky chimney.
author: Unknown

quote: The three finest sights in the world: a field of ripe wheat, a ship in full sail, and the wife of a Mac Donnell with child.[8]
author: Unknown

quote: [8] This triad comes from the Glynns of Antrim, the Mac Donnells' district.
author: Unknown

quote: In our collection an arrangement of the Triads in certain groups, according to their contents, is discernible. Thus, the first sixty
author: one--of which, however, the opening thirty-one are no Triads at all--are all topographical; and among the rest, those dealing with legal matters stand out clearly (Secs. 149-172).

quote: When the collection was made we have no means of ascertaining, except from internal evidence, such as the age of the language, and a few allusions to events, the date of which we can approximately fix.
author: Unknown

quote: [9] I may mention particularly the relative forms _teite_ 167, _bite_ 127, _ata_ 75, 76, 224, &c., _berta_ (O. Ir. _berte_) 109, 110, _fichte_ (145), _coillte_ (166), _teite_ (167), _aragellat_ (sic leg. with N) 171; the deponent _neimthigedar_ 116, &c.; _ato_, 'I am' (104), and the use of the perfective _ad
author: _ in _conaittig_ 77, 78.

quote: The nasal stem _leimm_ makes its nom. plur. _leimmen_ in Old
author: Irish. In Sec. 32 we find instead (_tair-_)_leme_. So also _foimrimm_ makes its nom. plural _foimrimme_ in Sec. 163.

quote: The word _dorus_ is neuter in Old
author: Irish, making its nom. acc. plural either _dorus_ or _doirsea_. In our text (Secs. 173, 174) the word is masculine, and makes its nom. plural _doruis_.

quote: The neuter _grad_ in Sec. 166 makes its nom. plur. _gruda_ for O. Ir. _grad_.[11]
author: Unknown

quote: [11] The infinitive _bith_ for O. Ir. _buith_ (91), the dative _cinn_ for O. Ir. _ciunn_ (98, 135), the nom. pl. _sligthi_ for O. Ir. _sligid_ (which I have restored in Sec. 49), the confusion between _do_ and _di_ (e.g. 83), and other details are probably due to the Middle
author: and Modern-Irish transcribers.

quote: [12] See _Ancient Laws of Ireland_, vol, v., pp. 118

quote: Transcriber's Notes: Words in italics in the original are surrounded by _underscores_. Words in Greek in the original are transliterated and surrounded by +plus signs+. Variations in spelling, hyphenation, and presence or absence of accent marks remain as in the original.
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: One quotation in the original uses a macron over a vowel to indicate that the following letter should be an "n". In this text, the following representations are used:
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: [=e] represents "e" with a macron [=o] represents "o" with a macron [=u] represents "u" with a macron
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: A complete list of typographical corrections as well as other notes follows the text.
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: _MANY SAYINGS, WISE OR OTHERWISE, ON MANY SUBJECTS, GLEANED FROM MANY SOURCES_
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: AUTHOR OF "WAYSIDE SKETCHES," "MYTHLAND," "NATURAL HISTORY LORE AND LEGEND," "SYMBOLISM IN ART," "WILD FRUITS OF THE COUNTRYSIDE," "FAMILIAR WILD FLOWERS," ETC.
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: LONDON ELLIOT STOCK, 62 PATERNOSTER ROW, E.C. 1902
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: "Am I not strong and proverb'd for a fool In ev'ry street: do they not say, how well Are come upon him his deserts?"
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: Our readers will doubtless recall, too, how in Holy Writ it is declared that "Israel shall be a proverb and a byeword among all people."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: The word saw is Saxon in its origin, and is defined by our great lexicographer as "a saying, a maxim, a sentence, an axiom, a proverb." Shakespeare writes
"From the table of my memory I'll wipe away all saws of books,"
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: Milton writes, somewhat more forbiddingly, of
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: "Strict age and sour severity With their grave saws."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: The word proverb is Greek in its inception, and means, literally, a wayside saying. Adage, a fairly equivalent word, is also of Greek birth. The reference in "Macbeth" will at once be recalled
"Letting I dare not wait upon I would, Like the poor cat i' the adage."[5:A]
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: Horace declares, wisely enough, in his "Art of Poetry," in favour of brevity:
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: "Short be the precept which with care is gained By docile minds, and faithfully retained,"
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: and it is a very valuable feature. How happy in expression, for instance, are such proverbs as "Fast bind, fast find," "Forewarned, forearmed," "Haste is waste."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: A proverb that is figurative in its construction may be considered as really a condensed parable. Hence Chaucer, in the prologue of "The Wife of Bathe," refers to "eke the paraboles of Salomon."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: Numerous examples of these word
author: pictures will at once occur to us. How expressive of the reproductive power of evil is the well-known proverb, "Ill weeds grow apace." In one of the Harleian MSS. of about the year 1490 we find it given as "Ewyl weed ys sone y-growe"; or, as another writer hath it,--

quote: "Ill weede groweth fast, ales, whereby the corne is lorne, For surely the weed overgroweth the corn."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: In France it is "Mauvaise herbe croit toujours," and in Italy "Erba mala preste cresce," and in some kindred form it appears in the proverb lore of almost all people. How telling, again, the picture and the lesson in "Still waters run deep."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: Small griefs find tongues: full casks are ever found To give, if any, yet but little sound; Deep waters noiseless are, and this we know, That chiding streams betray small depth below.
author: -_Herrick._

quote: "There is a tide in the affairs of men,"
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: we are told by Shakespeare, who doubtless knew this proverb, and transmuted it by his genius into fine gold
"Which, taken at the flood, leads on to fortune; Omitted, all the voyage of their life Is bound in shallows and in miseries."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: "Pandarus, whiche that stode her faste by, Felt iron hotte, and he began to smite."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: Yet, remember, would one prosper, "Have not too many irons in the fire." These proverbs suggested by the smithy recall yet one other, and a very ancient one, "Inter malleum et incudem
author: -"Between the hammer and the anvil"--a position so hemmed in with danger that no way of escape seems possible.

quote: The Divine Teacher did not scruple to employ this form of speech. In the synagogue of Nazareth He reminds them of the popular proverb, "Physician, heal thyself," and at the Well of Sychar He declares that saying true, "One soweth, another reapeth."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: "Apt alliteration's artful aid" is sometimes invoked. Such precepts as "Live and let live," "No silver, no servant," "Like likes like," "Out of debt, out of danger," "Time tryeth troth,"[22:A] and "No cross, no crown," and the Latin, "In vino veritas,"[22:B] are the more easily retained in the memory in consequence.
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: [4:A] "A dictionary of the English language, in which the words are deduced from their originals, and illustrated in their different significations by examples from the best authors." This early edition is in two massive quarto volumes, and the later abridgments that are now alone seen give no conception of the value of the original work.
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: [10:A] A great use is made in the Bible of this thought
author: compelling antithesis. One illustration will suffice: "There is a shame that bringeth sin, and there is a shame that is glory and grace."

quote: "The stone that is rouling can gather no mosse, Who often remooueth is sure of losse, The riche it compelleth to paie for his pride; The poore it vndooeth on euerie side."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: [11:A] "In manye suche parablis he spak to hem the word, as thei myghten here, and he spak not to hem withoute parable.
author: -_Wiclif's translation of the Bible._

quote: The holye scripture hath her figure and historye, her mysterye and veritie, her parable and playne doctryne.
author: -_Bale._

quote: [14:A] "Or the golden bowl be broken, or the pitcher broken at the cistern.
author: -_Ecclesiastes_ xii. 6.

quote: [14:B] "Ou ne fait point d'omelettes sans casser les oeufs," says the Frenchman, and the Spaniard agrees, "No se hacen tortillas sin romper huevos."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: My reasoning your reason setteth nought by, But reason for reason yee so stiffly lay, By proverbe for proverbe that with you doe way.
author: -_Heywood._

quote: [22:A] Nearly equivalent to the delightful saying of classic times, "Veritas temporis filia."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: [22:B] To some extent preserved in the modern equivalents, "La verdad esta en el vino," "Dans le vin on dit la verite."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: [23:A] In modern Portuguese, "O homem impoem, Deus dispoem."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: [23:B] In modern Portuguese, "Duro com duro nao faz bom muro."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: The priceless gift of wisdom, far in value above rubies, is dwelt upon and enforced, and its saving strength referred to time after time. The wisdom enshrined in this book, if incorporated in the heart and illuminating the life, would suffice as a complete _vade mecum_.
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: "O Salomon, richest of all richesse, Fulfilled of sapience and worldly glorie, Ful worthy ben thy wordes to memorie To every wight that wit and reason can."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: CHAUCER, _The Marchantes Tale_.
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: In the year 1676 one Henry D'Anvers collated these proverbs, and arranged them in alphabetical sequence as an aid to the memory. He entitled it "A Presentation of the Proverbs of Solomon in English Dress." At the opening of the book the writer, as in the Biblical original, seeks wisdom. His search is at first fruitless:
"Fare wel (said I), for yet it don't appear, That Wisdom (whom I seek for) dwelleth here. So I departed thence with speedy feet, When as I found that was not Wisdom's seat."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: At last, however, he seeks it in the Bible, and his perseverance is here rewarded:
"She's glorious within, enlightened eyes Do see such beauty which they can't but prize. She hath one room all hung with Pearls (you'll see), King Solomon's Proverbs, full of dignity."
author: Hulme, F. Edward (Frederick Edward)

quote: Aristotle[1] divides the blessings of life into three classes
author: -those which come to us from without, those of the soul, and those of the body. Keeping nothing of this division but the number, I observe that the fundamental differences in human lot may be reduced to three distinct classes:

quote: [Footnote 1: _Eth. Nichom_., I. 8.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: (1) What a man is: that is to say, personality, in the widest sense of the word; under which are included health, strength, beauty, temperament, moral character, intelligence, and education.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: (2) What a man has: that is, property and possessions of every kind.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: Cf. Clemens Alex. Strom. II., 21.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Volk und Knecht und Uberwinder Sie gestehen, zu jeder Zeit, Höchtes Glück der Erdenkinder Sei nur die Persönlichkeit_.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Gemmas, marmor, ebur, Tyrrhena sigilla, tabellas, Argentum, vestes, Gaetulo murice tinctas Sunt qui non habeant, est qui non curat habere_;
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: and when Socrates saw various articles of luxury spread out for sale, he exclaimed: _How much there is in the world I do not want_.

quote: It is a great piece of folly to sacrifice the inner for the outer man, to give the whole or the greater part of one's quiet, leisure and independence for splendor, rank, pomp, titles and honor. This is what Goethe did. My good luck drew me quite in the other direction.

author: -a diviner air, where the gods live serene.

quote: Les Maximes de la Rochefoucauld sont des proverbs des gens d'esprit.

quote: Maxims are the condensed good sense of nations.

quote: *In all the French editions this book is spoken of as published in 1693. The only copy I have seen is in the Cambridge University Library, 47, 16, 81, and is called "Reflexions Morales."
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: The fifth count, one of the ministers of Louis XIII., after fighting against the English and Buckingham at the Ile de Re, was created a duke. His son Francis, the second duke, by his writings has made the family name a household word.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: The fifth duke, banished from Court by Louis XV., became the friend of the philosopher Voltaire.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: But Richelieu of course discovered this plot, and Rochefoucauld was, of course, sent to the Bastile. He was liberated after a week's imprisonment, but banished to his chateau at Verteuil.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: The reason for this clemency was that the Cardinal desired to win Rochefoucauld from the Queen's party. A command in the army was offered to him, but by the Queen's orders refused.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: For some three years Rochefoucauld remained at Verteuil, waiting the time for his reckoning with Richelieu; speculating on the King's death, and the favours he would then receive from the Queen. During this period he was more or less engaged in plotting against his enemy the Cardinal, and hatching treason with Cinq Mars and De Thou.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: This completed Rochefoucauld's active life.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: When he recovered his health, he devoted himself to society. Madame de Sable assumed a hold over him. He lived a quiet life, and occupied himself in composing an account of his early life, called his "Memoirs," and his immortal "Maxims."
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: From the time he ceased to take part in public life, Rochefoucauld's real glory began. Having acted the various parts of soldier, politician, and lover with but small success, he now commenced the part of moralist, by which he is known to the world.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: Dr. Johnson speaks of it as "the only book written by a man of fashion, of which professed authors need be jealous."
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: "I love my friends; and I love them to such an extent that I would not for a moment weigh my interest against theirs. I condescend to them, I patiently endure their bad temper. But, also, I do not make much of their caresses, and I do not feel great uneasiness in their absence.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: Such is his own description of himself. Let us now turn to the other picture, delineated by the man who was his bitterest enemy, and whom (we say it with regret) Rochefoucauld tried to murder.
author: La Rochefoucauld, François duc de

quote: As to the author, M. Sainte Beuve says:
author: -"C'etait un misanthrope poli, insinuant, souriant, qui precedait de bien peu et preparait avec charme l'autre MISANTHROPE."

quote: As to the book, Mr. Hallam says:
author: -"Among the books in ancient and modern times which record the conclusions of observing men on the moral qualities of their fellows, a high place should be reserved for the Maxims of Rochefoucauld".

quote: Is there anything whereof it may be said, See, this is new! It hath been already of old time, Which was before us. There is no remembrance of former things; Neither shall there be any remembrance Of things that are to come With those that shall come after.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: But in spite of the fact that the Ancient Egyptians enjoy rather more popularity than their contemporaries, it is evident that the books which they wrote are closed books to those who have not the glamour of vanished peoples, and the fascination of mighty cities now made desolate, strong upon them.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: Nor do the oldest books of any other country approach these two in antiquity. To draw {20} comparisons between them let us, in imagination, place ourselves at the period at which Ptah
author: hotep lived, that is, about B.C. 3550, 'under King Isosi, living for ever,' and take a glance at futurity.

quote: The characters are clearly and carefully made, but are not always correct, as though the copyist had a badly written or very cursive copy before him, and was not always sure of his spelling.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: Duties toward superiors (Ph. 2, 7, 8, 10, 16, 27, 31).

quote: Not what he was, but what he should have been.

quote: As regards the version here offered, I will only say that it has been done with considerable care, {39} without prejudice, and, it is thought, in accordance with scientific methods of translation; and that it has been compared with all previous renderings, and will be found to be, on the whole, the most accurate that has yet appeared.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: _Those that built them tombs_, he sang, _have now no resting
author: place. Lo! what of their deeds? I have heard the words of Yemhotep and of Hardedef, whose sayings men repeat continually. Behold! where are their abodes? Their walls are over-thrown, and their places are not, even as though they had not been._'
