author: -Manners for Guests--From Father to Son--A Just Judge--The Treatment of Servants--Duties of the Great--The Test of Friendship--The Beauty of Obedience--One Generation to Another--Whom the King Honoureth

quote: The Instruction of the Governor of his City, the Vizier, Ptah
author: hotep, in the Reign of the King of Upper and Lower Egypt, Isosi, living for ever, to the end of Time.

quote: Said the Majesty of this God:[1] 'Instruct him, then, in the words of old time; may he be a wonder unto the children of princes, that they may enter and hearken with him. Make straight all their hearts; and discourse with him, without causing weariness.'
author: Ptahhotep

quote: 1. Be not proud because thou art learned; but discourse with the ignorant man, as with the sage. For no limit can be set to skill, neither is there any craftsman that possesseth full advantages. Fair speech is more rare than the emerald that is found by slave
author: maidens on the pebbles.

quote: 3. If thou find an arguer talking, thy fellow, one that is within thy reach, keep not silence when he saith aught that is evil; so shalt thou be wiser than he. Great will be the applause on the part of the listeners, and thy name shall be good in the knowledge of princes.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: 9. If thou have ploughed, gather thine harvest in the field, and the God shall make it great under thine hand. Fill not thy mouth at thy neighbours' table....[6] If a crafty man be the {46} possessor of wealth, he stealeth like a crocodile from the priests.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: Let not a man be envious that hath no children; let him be neither downcast nor quarrelsome on account of it. For a father, though great, may be grieved; as to the mother of children, she hath less peace than another. Verily, each man is created [to his destiny] by the God, Who is the chief of a tribe, trustful in following him.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: 10. If thou be lowly, serve a wise man, that all thine actions may be good before the God. If thou have known a man of none account that hath been advanced in rank, be not haughty toward him on account of that which thou knowest concerning him; but honour him that hath been advanced, according to that which he hath become.
author: Ptahhotep

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK THE ESSAYS OF ARTHUR SCHOPENHAUER; COUNSELS AND MAXIMS ***
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Le bonheur n'est pas chose aisée: il est très difficile de le trouver en nous, et impossible de le trouver ailleurs_.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: INTRODUCTION I. GENERAL RULES II. OUR RELATION TO OURSELVES III. OUR RELATION TO OTHERS IV. WORLDLY FORTUNE V. THE AGES OF LIFE
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: I refer to the proverbs and maxims ascribed, in the Old Testament, to the king of that name.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: The first and foremost rule for the wise conduct of life seems to me to be contained in a view to which Aristotle parenthetically refers in the _Nichomachean Ethics_:[1] [Greek: o phronimoz to alupon dioke e ou to aedu] or, as it may be rendered, _not pleasure, but freedom from pain, is what the wise man will aim at_.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: vii. (12) 12.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: _Welt als Wille und Vorstellung_. Vol. I., p. 58.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: One might well fancy that these visions of wishes fulfilled were the work of some evil spirit, conjured up in order to entice us away from that painless state which forms our highest happiness.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: _Welt als Wille und Vorstellung_, vol. ii., ch. 16.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Auream quisquis mediocritatem Diligit, tutus caret obsoleti Sordibus tecti, caret invidenda Sobrius aula. Savius ventis agitatur ingens Pinus: et celsae graviori casu Decidunt turres; feriuntque summos Fulgura monies.[2]_
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: Letters to and from Merck.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 2: Horace. Odes II. x.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Though from thy grasp all worldly things should flee, Grieve not for them, for they are nothing worth: And though a world in thy possession be, Joy not, for worthless are the things of earth. Since to that better world 'tis given to thee To pass, speed on, for this is nothing worth._[2]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: _Republic_, x. 604.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 2: _Translator's Note_. From the Anvár
author: i Suhailí--_The Lights of Canopus_--being the Persian version of the _Table of Bidpai_. Translated by E.B. Eastwick, ch. iii. Story vi., p. 289.]

quote: SECTION 2. To estimate a man's condition in regard to happiness, it is necessary to ask, not what things please him, but what things trouble him; and the more trivial these things are in themselves, the happier the man will be. To be irritated by trifles, a man must be well off; for in misfortunes trifles are unfelt.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _quid eternis minorem Consiliis animum fatigas?_[1]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: Odes II. xi.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: The cause of this commonest of all follies is that optical illusion of the mind from which everyone suffers, making life, at its beginning, seem of long duration; and at its end, when one looks back over the course of it, how short a time it seems! There is some advantage in the illusion; but for it, no great work would ever be done.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Altro diletto che 'mparar, non provo_.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: It may even be that they to some extent still follow their old wishes and aims, trifling with them, as it were, for the sake of appearances; all the while really and seriously looking for nothing but instruction; a process which lends them an air of genius, a trait of something contemplative and sublime.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: In their search for gold, the alchemists discovered other things
author: -gunpowder, china, medicines, the laws of nature. There is a sense in which we are all alchemists.

quote: OUR RELATION TO OURSELVES.
author: -SECTION 4.

quote: The mason employed on the building of a house may be quite ignorant of its general design; or at any rate, he may not keep it constantly in mind. So it is with man: in working through the days and hours of his life, he takes little thought of its character as a whole.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Greek: alla ta men protuchthai easomen achnumenoi per tumhon eni staethessi philon damasntes hanankae],[1]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: and of the future as of that which lies beyond our power, in the lap of the gods
[Greek: all aetoi men tauta theon en gounasi keitai.][2]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: _Iliad_, xix, 65.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 2: _Ibid_, xvii, 514]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: But in regard to the present let us remember Seneca's advice, and live each day as if it were our whole life,
author: -_singulas dies singulas vitas puta_: let us make it as agreeable as possible, it is the only real time we have.

quote: [Footnote 1: _Translator's Note_. A series of Greek, Latin and French classics published at Zweibräcken in the Palatinate, from and after the year 1779. Cf. Butter, _Ueber die Bipontiner und die editiones Bipontinae_.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: _Eudem. Eth_. VII. ii. 37.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: For while a man cannot reckon with certainty upon anyone but himself, the burdens and disadvantages, the dangers and annoyances, which arise from having to do with others, are not only countless but unavoidable.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: As our body is concealed by the clothes we wear, so our mind is veiled in lies. The veil is always there, and it is only through it that we can sometimes guess at what a man really thinks; just as from his clothes we arrive at the general shape of his body.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: _Paradoxa Stoidorum_: II.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Cercato ho sempre solitaria vita (Le rive il sanno, e le campagne e i boschi) Per fuggir quest' ingegni storti e loschi Che la strada del ciel' hanno smarrita_.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Herodes ist ein Feind; der Joseph der Verstand, Dem machte Gott die Gefahr im Traum (in Geist) bekannt; Die Welt ist Bethlehem, Aegypten Einsamkeit, Fleuch, meine Seele! fleuch, sonst stirbest du vor Leid_.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: _Translator's Note_. Angelus Silesius, pseudonym for Johannes Scheffler, a physician and mystic poet of the seventeenth century (1624

Giordano Bruno also declares himself a friend of seclusion. _Tanti uomini_, he says, _che in terra hanno voluto gustare vita celeste, dissero con una voce, "ecce elongavi fugiens et mansi in solitudine_
author: -those who in this world have desired a foretaste of the divine life, have always proclaimed with one voice:

quote: _Lo! then would I wander far off; I would lodge in the wilderness._[1]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: Psalms, lv. 7.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Die Einsamkeit ist noth: doch sei nur nicht gemein, So kannst du überall in einer Wüste sein_.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: _Hör' auf mit deinem Gram zu spielen, Der, wie ein Geier, dir am Leben frisst: Die schlechteste Gesellschaft lässt dich fühlen Dass du ein Mensch mit Menschen bist.[1]_
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: Goethe's _Faust_, Part I., 1281
[Footnote 1: This restricted, or, as it were, entrenched kind of sociability has been dramatically illustrated in a play
author: -well worth reading--of Moratin's, entitled _El Café o sea la Comedia Nuova_ (The Cafe or the New Comedy), chiefly by one of the characters, Don Pedro and especially in the second and third scenes of the first act.]

quote: [Footnote 1: Envy shows how unhappy people are; and their constant attention to what others do and leave undone, how much they are bored.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 2: _De Ira_: iii., 30.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: The members of one of these aristocracies usually get on very well with those of another, and there is no call for envy between them, because their several privileges effect an equipoise.
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: [Footnote 1: It may be observed, in passing, that a great many of the maxims which Goethe puts under the head of _Proverbial_, are translations from the Italian.]
author: Schopenhauer, Arthur

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK A COLLECTION OF LATIN MAXIMS AND PHRASES LITERALLY TRANSLATED ***
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: A COLLECTION OF LATIN MAXIMS AND PHRASES =Literally Translated.= INTENDED FOR THE USE OF STUDENTS FOR ALL LEGAL EXAMINATIONS.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: BY JOHN N. COTTERELL, SOLICITOR AND NOTARY PUBLIC.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: “Scire leges: non est verba earum tenere, sed vim ac mentem.”
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: “Maxima ita dicto quia maxima est ejus dignitas, et certissima auctoritas, atque quod maximè omnibus probetur.”
author: _Co. Litt._, 1 Inst. xi.

quote: LONDON: STEVENS AND HAYNES, =Law Publishers,= BELL YARD, TEMPLE BAR. 1913.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: Those maxims only have been selected which are constantly met with by the student, and which he would do well to commit to memory; leading cases are also referred to. The explanations have been made as brief as possible, and for deeper research the student is referred to Broom’s Legal Maxims.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: PREFACE TO SECOND AND THIRD EDITIONS.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: After a busy and practical experience of many years the writer can now in all earnestness
author: as during the days of studentship he did in all distrust and doubtfulness—emulate the writer of old who said—

quote: “Cognitio legis est copulata et complicata.”
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: Our greatest writers of more recent years have also recognised the intricate and ever
author: changing study of the Law. The late Lord Tennyson, in that most beautiful poem, “Aylmer’s Field,” tells us—

quote: Those who wish to follow successfully the law as a profession must remain students to the last, and the leading truths and time
author: honoured legal principles, as defined by the maxims hereafter contained, will ever serve alike as safe landmarks, and sheet anchors, in times of doubt and uncertainty.

quote: Since the publication of the First Edition, the number of maxims (very properly defined as the condensed good sense of nations) has been considerably enlarged, but the student will find the more important ones prefixed by an asterisk, and these may with advantage be memorized.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: A COLLECTION OF LATIN MAXIMS AND PHRASES.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 1. A verbis legis non recedendum est.= _The words of the law must not be departed from._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 2. Accessorium non ducit sed sequitur suum principale.= _The accessory does not lead but follows its principal._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: The grant of a reversion will also include a rent incident thereto
author: so heir-looms follow the inheritance.

quote: =3. Accusare nemo se debet, nisi coram Deo.= _No one is in duty bound to accuse himself unless before God._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: In certain cases a witness is not compelled to answer, if by so doing he would incriminate himself. (See Max. No. 171.)
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 4. Acta exteriora indicant interiora secreta.= _Overt acts make known latent thoughts_, or _Acts indicate the intention_.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: Where an authority given by law is abused, the person becomes a trespasser _ab initio_, but not so if authority be given by party, or in cases of mere non
author: feasance. (_Six Carpenters’ Case_, 1 Smith, L. C. 11th ed. p. 132.)

quote: =* 5. Actio personalis moritur cum personâ.= _A personal action dies with the person._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =6. Actus curiae neminem gravabit.= _The act of the Court shall prejudice no man._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: (_Cumber_ v. _Wane_, 1 Sm. L. C. 11th ed. p. 338.)
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 7. Actus Dei nemini facit injuriam.= _The act of God causes injury to no one._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: Storms, tempests, and the like, are acts of God, being inevitable accidents not caused by man.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =8. Actus me invito, non est meus actus.= _An involuntary act is not one’s own act_, i.e., _an act done against one’s will is not such person’s act_.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: The law presumes coercion in certain cases
author: by a husband over his wife. Intentions denominate the action, and especially so in criminal cases. (See next Max. and Nos. 116 and 285.)

quote: =* 9. Actus non facit reum, nisi mens sit rea.= _The act itself does not make a man guilty, unless his intention be so._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: There must be a vicious will or criminal intention as well as an unlawful act. (See Maxs. Nos. 8, 116 and 285.) Where one engaged in doing a lawful act, without any wrongful intention, unfortunately and inadvertently kills another person, the homicide is excusable.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =10. Ad questiones facti non respondent judices; ad questiones legis non respondent juratores.= _Judges do not decide questions of fact; the jury do not decide questions of law._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: This applies to trials by jury, and where the issue turns rather upon facts than legal construction, such method of trial is usually, but not necessarily, followed.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 11. Aequitas factum habet quod fieri oportuit.= _Equity looks upon that as done which ought to have been done._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =12. Aequitas nunquam contravenit leges.= _Equity never opposes the law._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: To supplement, and not to contravene, is its object.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 13. Aequitas sequitur legem.= _Equity follows the law. Equity cannot alter the law of the land, but follows it._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: Both in the sense of obeying the law, and conforming to its general rules and policy, and also in applying to equitable estates and interests the rules by which at common law legal estates and interests of a similar kind are governed.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =14. Agentes et consentientes pari poenâ plectentur.= _Acting and consenting parties are liable to the same punishment._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 15. Alienatio rei praefertur juri accrescendi.= _The law favours alienation rather than accumulation._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =16. Allegans contraria non est audiendus.= _One who contradicts himself is not to be heard._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: A rule of evidence relative to the credibility of a witness. Cross
author: examination is frequently used to this end.

quote: =17. Allegans suam turpitudinem non est audiendus.= _A person boasting of his own wrong
author: doing is not to be heard._

quote: =* 18. Ambiguitas contra stipulatorem est.= _An ambiguity is taken against the party using it._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: Thus, if in a lease, words of exception be used ambiguously, the same being words of the lessor, are construed most strongly as against him. (See Chitty on Contracts, 16th ed. p. 113; also Max. No. 272.)
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 19. Ambiguitas verborum latens verificatione suppletur; nam quod ex facto oritur ambiguum verificatione facti tollitur.= _A hidden ambiguity of the words may be interpreted by evidence; for an ambiguity which arises from an extrinsic fact may be removed by proof of such fact._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =* 20. Ambiguitas verborum patens nullâ verificatione excluditur.= _A patent ambiguity of the words cannot be removed by extrinsic evidence._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =21. Amicus curie.= _A friend to the Court_, i.e., _one who advises disinterestedly and spontaneously_.
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: =22. Aqua cedit solo.= _Water passes with the soil._
author: Cotterell, John N.

quote: From a legal point of view, water is land covered by water, and an action cannot be brought to recover possession of a pool, &c., by the name of water only, but as so much land covered by water. Water, being a movable thing, must continue common, and its ownership therefore goes with the land below.
author: Cotterell, John N.

author: tenth part of their time to be employed in its service: but idleness taxes many of us much more; sloth, by bringing on diseases, absolutely shortens life.

author: day it rains and I cannot work.]

author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: And again, "Three removes are as bad as a fire," and again, "Keep thy shop, and thy shop will keep thee:" and again, "If you would have your business done, go; if not, send." And again,
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: "He that by the plow would thrive, Himself must either hold or drive."
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: 'And again, "The eye of the master will do more work than both his hands:" and again, "Want of care does us more damage than want of knowledge;" and again, "Not to oversee workmen, is to leave them your purse open."
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: III. 'So much for industry, my friends, and attention to one's own business; but to these we must add frugality, if we would make our industry more certainly successful. A man may if he knows not how to save as he gets, "keep his nose all his life to the grindstone, and die not worth a groat at last. A fat kitchen makes a lean will;" and,
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: "Many estates are spent in the getting, Since women for tea forsook spinning and knitting, And men for punch forsook hewing and splitting."
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: If you would be wealthy, think of saving, as well as of getting. The Indies have not made Spain rich, because her out
author: goes are greater than her incomes."

quote: 'Away, then, with your expensive follies, and you will not then have so much cause to complain of hard times, heavy taxes, and chargeable families; for,

quote: "Women and wine, game and deceit, Make the wealth small, and the want great."

author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: "Vessels large may venture more, But little boats should keep near shore."
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: "For age and want save while you may, No morning sun lasts a whole day."
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: Gain may be temporary and uncertain; but ever, while you live, expense is constant and certain; and "It is easier to build two chimneys, than to keep one in fuel," as Poor Richard says: so, "Rather go to bed supper
author: less, than rise in debt,"

quote: Get what you can, and what you get hold, 'Tis the stone that will turn all your lead into gold.
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: And when you have got the Philosopher's stone, sure you will no longer complain of bad times, or the difficulty of paying taxes.
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: W. and T. Darton, Printers, Holborn
author: Hill, London.

quote: Only the most obvious and clear punctuation errors repaired. The opening single quotes end pages later.
author: Franklin, Benjamin

quote: _BEHOLD, the days come, saith the Lord God, that I will send a famine in the land, not a famine of bread, nor a thirst for water, but of hearing the words of the Lord._
author: Unknown

quote: _THEN said they unto him, Tell us, we pray thee, ... what is thine occupation? and whence comest thou? what is thy country? and of what people art thou?_
author: Unknown

quote: _And he said unto them, I am an Hebrew; and I fear the Lord, the God of heaven, which hath made the sea and the dry land._
author: Unknown

quote: YE are My witnesses, saith the Lord, and My servant whom I have chosen.
author: Unknown

quote: EVERY Hebrew should look upon his Faith as a temple extending over every land to prove the immutability of God and the unity of His purposes. He should regard himself as one of the pillars which support that temple from falling to the ground; and add, however insignificant in itself, to the strength, the durability, and the beauty of the whole.
author: Unknown

quote: ELEAZAR (ROKËACH) OF WORMS, c. 1200. (_Trans. M. Joseph._)
author: Unknown

quote: EVERY ISRAELITE HOLDS THE HONOUR OF HIS ENTIRE PEOPLE IN HIS HANDS
author: Unknown

quote: WE Jews have a more pressing responsibility for our lives and beliefs than perhaps any other religious community.
author: Unknown

quote: Visit the sick and suffering man, and let thy countenance be cheerful when he sees it, but not so that thou oppress the helpless one with gaiety. Comfort those that are in grief; let piety where thou seest it affect thee even to tears; and then it may be that thou wilt be spared the grief of weeping over the death of thy children.
author: Unknown

quote: Respect the poor man by gifts whose hand he knows not of; be not deaf to his beseechings, deal not hard words out to him, and give him of thy richest food when he sits at meat with thee.
author: Unknown

quote: From a wicked neighbour, see that thou keep aloof, and spend not much of thy time among the people who speak ill of their brother
author: man; be not as the fly that is always seeking sick and wounded places; and tell not of the faults and failings of those about thee.

quote: Never enter thy house with abrupt and startling step, and bear not thyself so that those who dwell under thy roof shall dread when in thy presence. Purge thy soul of angry passion, that inheritance of fools; love wise men, and strive to know more and more of the works and the ways of the Creator.
author: Unknown

quote: BE not ready to quarrel; avoid oaths and passionate adjurations, excess of laughter and outbursts of wrath; they disturb and confound the reason of man. Avoid all dealings wherein there is a lie; utter not the name of God superfluously, or in places dirty or defiled.
author: Unknown

quote: Cut from under thee all mere human supports, and make not gold the foremost longing of thy life; for that is the first step to idolatry. Rather give money than words; and as to ill words, see that thou place them in the scale of understanding before they leave thy lips.
author: Unknown

quote: What has been uttered in thy presence, even though not told as secret, let it not pass from thee to others. And if one tell thee a tale, say not to him that thou hast heard it all before. Do not fix thine eyes too much on one who is far above thee in wealth, but on those who are behind thee in worldly fortune.
author: Unknown

quote: Put no one to open shame; misuse not thy power against any one; who can tell whether thou wilt not some day be powerless thyself?
author: Unknown

quote: Do not struggle vaingloriously for the small triumph of showing thyself in the right and a wise man in the wrong; thou art not one whit the wiser therefor. Be not angry or unkind to any one for trifles, lest thou make thyself enemies unnecessarily.
author: Unknown

quote: THE Jew’s home has rarely been his ‘castle’. Throughout the ages it has been something far higher――his sanctuary.
author: Unknown

quote: BE careful not to cause woman to weep, for God counts her tears. Israel was redeemed from Egypt on account of the virtue of its women. He who weds a good woman, it is as if he had fulfilled all the precepts of the Law.
author: Unknown

quote: THE thread on which the different good qualities of human beings are strung as pearls, is the fear of God. When the fastenings of this fear are unloosed, the pearls roll in all directions and are lost one by one.
author: Unknown

quote: BOOK OF MORALS, 15th cent.
author: Unknown

quote: THE knowledge of Hebrew is the golden hinge upon which our national and religious existence turns. Flowing down from the hills of eternity, the Hebrew language has been set apart by God as the receptacle of truths destined to sway mankind and humanize the world.
author: Unknown

quote: Sympathetic appreciation of this indomitable type, this harmonious albeit rugged personality, might well be taken as a touchstone of a man’s mentality, culture, and humanity.
author: Unknown

quote: THE STUDENT OF THE TORAH[3]
author: Unknown

quote: IF one asks a student to
author: day why he studies, he will at once, in spite of his youth, give a very practical answer. He mentions the profession for which he is preparing himself, and through which he will obtain a lucrative office or a comfortable position in life.

quote: O MY God, and God of My Fathers,
author: Unknown

quote: ON THE THRESHOLD OF MANHOOD
author: Unknown

quote: RABBI Hanina[5], son of Dosa, said: ‘He in whom the fear of sin comes before wisdom, his wisdom shall endure; but he in whom wisdom comes before the fear of sin, his wisdom will not endure.’
author: Unknown

quote: I BELIEVE in God, the One and Holy, the Creator and Sustainer of the world.
author: Unknown

quote: I believe that man possesses a Divine power wherewith he may subdue his evil impulses and passions, strive to come nearer and nearer the perfection of God, and commune with Him in prayer.
author: Unknown

quote: I believe that select individuals are, from time to time, called by God as prophets and charged with the mission of declaring His will unto men.
author: Unknown

quote: I believe that man is subject to God’s law and responsible to the Searcher of the human heart and the Righteous Judge for all his thoughts and deeds.
author: Unknown

quote: I believe that he who confesses his sins and turns from his evil ways and truly repents is lovingly forgiven by his Father in Heaven.
author: Unknown

quote: I believe that the pious who obey God’s law and do His will with a perfect heart, and those who truly repent, share, as immortal souls, in the everlasting life of God.
author: Unknown

quote: JUDAISM A POSITIVE RELIGION
author: Unknown

quote: A CLEAR and concise definition of Judaism[7] is very difficult to give, for the reason that it is not a religion pure and simple based upon accepted creeds, but is one inseparably connected with the Jewish nation as the depositary and guardian of the truths held by it for mankind.
author: Unknown

quote: Far from having become 1,900 years ago a stagnant religion, Judaism has ever remained ‘a river of God full of living waters’, which, while running within the river
author: bed of a single nation, has continued to feed anew the great streams of human civilization.

quote: SOLOMON IBN GABIROL, 1050. (_Trans. M. Jastrow._)
author: Unknown

quote: I CALL heaven and earth to witness that whether it be Jew or heathen, man or woman, free or bondman――only according to their acts does the Divine spirit rest upon them.
author: Unknown

quote: SALVATION is attained not by subscription to metaphysical dogmas, but solely by love of God that fulfils itself in action. This is a cardinal truth in Judaism.
author: Unknown

quote: OF THE PROJECT GUTENBERG EBOOK THE OFFICER'S MANUAL: NAPOLEON'S MAXIMS OF WAR ***
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: NAPOLEON’S MAXIMS OF WAR.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: RICHMOND, VA.: WEST & JOHNSTON. 1862.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: EVANS & COGSWELL, PRINTERS. NO. 3 BROAD ST., CHARLESTON, S. C.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: “The first is the same in all ages, and with all nations, whatever be the arms with which they fight. Hence it follows that, in every age, great commanders have been governed by the same principles.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: “The business of detail, on the contrary, is controlled by existing circumstances. It varies with the character of a people, and the quality of their arms.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: “It is with a view to impress the justice of this remark, that I have sought for facts in different periods of history, to illustrate these maxims, and to prove that nothing is _problematical_ in war; but that failure and success in military operations depend almost always on the natural genius and science of the chief.”
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: The frontiers of states are either large rivers, or chains of mountains, or deserts. Of all these obstacles to the march of an army, the most difficult to overcome is the desert; mountains come next, and broad rivers occupy the third place.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Napoleon, in his military career, appears to have been called upon to surmount every difficulty which can occur in wars of invasion.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: In Egypt he traversed deserts, and vanquished and destroyed the Mamelukes, so celebrated for their address and courage. His genius knew how to accommodate itself to all the dangers of this distant enterprise, in a country ill adapted to supply the wants of his troops.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: In forming the plan of a campaign, it is requisite to foresee everything the enemy may do, and to be prepared with the necessary means to counteract it.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Plans of campaign may be modified _ad infinitum_ according to circumstances
author: -the genius of the general, the character of the troops, and the topography of the theatre of action.

quote: An army which undertakes the conquest of a country, has its two wings resting either upon neutral territories, or upon great natural obstacles, such as rivers or chains of mountains. It happens in some cases that only one wing is so supported; and in others that both are exposed.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: It was by the neglect of these principles, also, that Charles XII, abandoning his line of operation and all communication with Sweden, threw himself into the Ukraine, and lost the greater part of his army by the fatigue of a winter campaign in a barren country destitute of resources.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Defeated at Pultawa, he was obliged to seek refuge in Turkey, after crossing the Nieper with the remains of his army, diminished to little more than one thousand men.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: All wars should be governed by certain principles, for every war should have a definite object, and be conducted according to the rules of art. (A war should only be undertaken with forces proportioned to the obstacles to be overcome.)
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: The following maxims, taken from the memoirs of Montécuculli, appear to me well suited to this place, and calculated to form a useful commentary on the general principles laid down in the preceding maxim:
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: After having strictly conformed to all the rules of war, and satisfied ourselves that nothing has been omitted to ensure eventual success, we must then leave the issue in the hands of Providence, and repose ourselves tranquilly in the decision of a higher power.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Let what will arrive, it is the part of a general
author: in-chief to remain firm and constant in his purposes; he must not allow himself to be elated by prosperity, nor to be depressed by adversity: for in war good and bad and fortune succeed each other by turns, form the ebb and flow of military operations.

quote: 2. When your own army is strong and inured to service, and that of the enemy is weak and consists of new levies, or of troops enervated by long inaction, then you should exert every means to bring him to battle.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: A general can, therefore, rely only on the proper arrangement of his troops, to enable him to repel any attempt the adversary may make upon the front, or flanks, or rear of his army.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: If one flank of an army rests upon a river, or an impassable ravine, the whole of the cavalry may be posted with the other wing, in order to envelop the enemy more easily by its superiority in numbers.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: If the enemy has his flanks supported by woods, light cavalry or infantry should be despatched to attack him in flank or in rear during the heat of the battle. If practicable, also, an attack should be made upon the baggage, to add to his confusion.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: When a river is to be passed, the artillery should be placed in battery upon the bank opposite the point of crossing.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: The advanced guard of an army should be always provided with trusty guides, and with a corps of pioneers: the first to point out the best roads, the second to render these roads more practicable.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: From the moment an army approaches the enemy, it should march in the order in which it is intended to fight. If anything is to be apprehended, precautions are necessary in proportion to the degree of the danger. When a defile is to be passed, the troops should be halted beyond the extremity, until the whole army has quitted the defile.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: When a march is made to force a pass guarded by the enemy, it is desirable to make a feint upon one point, while, by a rapid movement, you bring your real attack to bear upon another.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: When an army occupies a fixed camp, it is necessary to be well supplied with provisions and ammunition, or at least that these should be within certain reach and easily obtained. To insure this, the line of communication must be well established, and care taken not to leave an enemy’s fortress in your rear.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: The winter quarters of an army should be protected, likewise, by constructing small covered works on all the lines of approach to the cantonments, and by posting advanced guards of cavalry to observe the motions of the enemy.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: If an enemy declines an engagement, he may be compelled to it, either by besieging a place of importance, or by falling upon him unawares, and when he cannot easily effect his retreat. Or (after pretending to retire), by making a rapid countermarch, attacking him vigorously and forcing him to action.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: To gain a battle, each arm must be advantageously posted, and have the means of engaging its front and in flank. The wings must be protected by natural obstacles, where these present themselves, or by having recourse when necessary to the aid of art.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: When it is necessary to attack with your whole force, the battle should commence toward evening; because then, whatever be the issue, night will arrive to separate the combatants before your troops are exhausted. By this means, an opportunity is afforded of affecting an orderly retreat if the result of the battle requires it.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: 9. In order to obtain every possible advantage from a diversion, we should ascertain first, that the country in which it is to be created is easily penetrated. A diversion should be made vigorously, and on those points where it is calculated to do the greatest mischief to the enemy.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: 10. To make war with success, the following principles should never be departed from:
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: It seems to me, however, that a general who should rest the success of a battle upon such a principle, would be more likely to lose than to gain by it; for if he had to deal with a skilful adversary and an alert tactician, the latter would find time to take advantage of the previous bad arrangements, before he would be able to remedy them.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: The strength of an army, like the power in mechanics, is estimated by multiplying the mass by the rapidity; a rapid march augments the _morale_ of an army, and increases its means of victory. Press on!
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Marshal Villars observes, that “in war everything depends upon being able to deceive the enemy; and having once gained this point, in never allowing him time to recover himself.” Villars has united practice to precept. His bold and rapid marches were almost always crowned with success.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: It was the opinion of Frederick that all wars should be short and rapid; because a long war insensibly relaxes discipline, depopulates the state, and exhausts its resources.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: An army ought to have only one line of operation. This should be preserved with care, and never abandoned but in the last extremity.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: The distances permitted between corps of an army upon the march must be governed by the localities, by circumstances, and by the object in view.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Such was Moreau’s retreat after the passage of the Adda by the Austro
author: Russian army. The French general, after having covered the evacuation of Milan, took up a position between the Po and the Tanaro.

quote: His camp rested upon Alexandria and Valentia, two capital fortresses, and had the advantage of covering the roads to Turin and Savona, by which he could effect his retreat in case he was unable to accomplish a junction with the _corps d’armee_ of Macdonald, who had been ordered to quit the kingdom of Naples, and hasten his march into Tuscany.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: To effect this object, he directed the whole of his battering train and heavy baggage by the Col de Fenestrelle upon France; then opening himself a way over the St. Bernard, he gained Loano with his light artillery and the small proportion of field equipment he had been able to preserve.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: By this skilful movement, he not only retained his communications with France, but was enabled to observe the motions of the army from Naples, and to facilitate his junction with it by directing the whole of his force upon the points necessary for that purpose.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Macdonald, in the meantime, whose only chance of success depended on concentrating his little army, neglected this precaution, and was beaten in three successive actions at the Trebia.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: In 1645, the French army, under the orders of the Prince of Condé, was on the march to lay siege to Nordlingen, when it was discovered that Count Merci, who commanded the Bavarians, had foreseen this intention, and had entrenched himself in a strong position which defended Nordlingen at the same time that it covered Donawerth.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: In a war of march and manœuvre, if you would avoid a battle with a superior army, it is necessary to entrench every night, and occupy a good defensive position. Those natural positions which are ordinarily met with, are not sufficient to protect an army against superior numbers without recourse to art.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Upon this, Montécuculli made a movement with his whole army, threatening the bridge at Ottenheim, by which the French received their provisions from upper Alsace.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: In the meantime, this attempt of the enemy having convinced the French general of the danger to which his bridge had exposed him, removed it nearer to that of Strasburg, in order to diminish the extent of ground he had to defend.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French

quote: Montécuculli, having commanded the magistrates of Strasburg to collect materials for a bridge, moved to Scherzheim to receive them; but Turenne again defeated his projects by taking a position at Freistett, where he occupied the islands of the Rhine, and immediately constructed a stockade.
author: Napoleon I, Emperor of the French
